selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As an 83-year-old man living in Rome, retired and with a life rich in experiences from raising four children and immigrating here, I've seen how the world has changed, especially with social media. I spend about an hour daily on platforms like Facebook, mostly to keep up with family photos and travel stories, but I've noticed more fake content popping up. Managing it starts with a simple, old-fashioned approach: trust your gut and don't believe everything you see. With my moderate openness, I'm curious but cautious; I learned long ago that not all information is reliable, much like stories passed down in my immigrant community. My low conscientiousness means I'm not overly systematic, so I rely on common sense—if a post seems too sensational or doesn't match what I know from my travels or painting, I question it. For example, I once saw a fake news story about a famous landmark in Rome, and I knew it was wrong because I've painted it myself. I think social media companies should do more to filter this stuff, but as users, we have a role too. I talk to my children about it, using our family chats to fact-check things, which helps given my low agreeableness—I'm not afraid to challenge what I see. My Christian beliefs guide me to seek truth and avoid spreading lies, so I rarely share posts unless I'm sure. In short, dealing with fake content is about staying skeptical, leaning on personal experience, and using social media wisely without letting it overwhelm you, which fits my good mental health and moderate neuroticism—I don't get too stressed by it.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age (83) suggests life experience and skepticism; gender (man) and sexual orientation (bisexual) not directly referenced; occupation (not applicable) and employment status (retired) imply time for reflection; city_of_living (Rome) and migration_status (immigrant) provide context for cultural awareness; education_level (lower secondary) indicates practical, non-academic approach; parents_education influences value of learning; current_marital_status (widowed) and children (4) suggest family-oriented perspective; psychological_feature (good mental health) supports balanced view; religious_beliefs (Christianity) emphasizes truth-seeking; time_onsocialmedia (one hour daily) limits exposure; hobbies (rock climbing, travel, painting) offer real-world examples; OCEAN traits: moderate openness (curious but cautious), low conscientiousness (reliance on intuition), moderate extraversion (social but selective), low agreeableness (willing to challenge), moderate neuroticism (manages stress). Opinion integrates these to advocate for personal vigilance and common sense in managing fake content.
